3 min read
Just now

--

Every limit you’ve hit on ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini? Tokens did that.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Ferhat Deniz Fors on UnsplashYou’ve probably seen the error: “You’ve reached your context limit.” Or noticed that a long conversation with an AI suddenly starts feeling… forgetful. Like it’s lost the thread.
That’s not a bug. That’s tokens doing their job — and hitting their ceiling.
Here’s what’s actually going on.
AI Doesn’t Read Words. It Reads Chunks.
When you type a message to an AI, it doesn’t process it the way you’d read a sentence. It breaks your text into small pieces called tokens.
Think of a token like a puzzle piece of language. Sometimes a token is a whole word. Sometimes it’s just part of one.
Here’s a rough idea of how it works:

• "hello" → 1 token

• "unbelievable" → 3–4 tokens (un / belie / vable)

• A space before a word? That’s often its own token too.
As a rough rule of thumb, 1 token ≈ 4 characters, or about ¾ of a word — so 1,000 words of text is roughly 1,300–1,500 tokens. That said, newer models use smarter tokenizers that compress text more efficiently, so the actual number keeps getting better.

Why Should You Care?
Because every AI model has a token limit — a cap on how much text it can process at once (both your input and its output combined).
While older models maxed out at 4K tokens, modern standards have exploded:

• GPT-4o handles 128K tokens as standard

• Claude handles up to 200K tokens

• Google’s Gemini has smashed the ceiling entirely with a 2 million token capacity
When you hit that limit, here’s what actually happens: in consumer apps like ChatGPT or Claude, the interface quietly drops your oldest messages to make room — so to you, it feels like the AI forgot, but it’s really the app filtering behind the scenes. For developers using the raw API, it’s a hard stop with an error code.
This is also why AI isn’t free to run. Providers charge per token — you’re essentially paying for how much the model has to think.
